explain.rule: The present perfect (have/has + past participle) connects the past to the present. Use it for: (1) life experiences with no specific time (Have you ever been to Japan?), (2) recent events with a present result (I've lost my keys — I can't get in), (3) unfinished time periods (I've drunk three coffees today — today isn't over), (4) with for + duration and since + starting point. Key signal words: ever, never, already, yet, just, recently, for, since, so far.
explain.why_matters: Present perfect is one of the most important tenses in English and one of the hardest for Hebrew speakers. It appears constantly in natural conversation. Getting it right is a clear sign of moving from A2 to B1.
explain.hebrew_note: Hebrew has no present perfect. Where English uses it, Hebrew uses past simple (עבר). 'I have been to Paris' = 'הייתי בפריז' in Hebrew — both look like past simple. The difference: if the time is NOT specified and the event is still relevant now → present perfect. If a specific past time is mentioned (yesterday, last year, in 2019) → past simple. This is the single biggest tense mistake Hebrew speakers make.
explain.tip: Think of present perfect as a bridge: the action happened in the past but it matters NOW. Test: can you answer 'when exactly'? If you have a specific time → past simple. If the time is unknown, unimportant, or up to now → present perfect. Also remember: NEVER use present perfect with yesterday, last week, in 2019, ago — those are past simple signals.

form.positive: I/you/we/they + have + past participle | he/she/it + has + past participle
form.negative: I/you/we/they + haven't + past participle | he/she/it + hasn't + past participle
form.question: Have + I/you/we/they + past participle? | Has + he/she/it + past participle?
form.short_answer: Yes, I have. / No, I haven't. | Yes, she has. / No, she hasn't.

key_words.example: Have you finished yet? / I haven't finished yet.
I've lived here for five years.

good_examples.note: Life experience — no specific time mentioned

good_examples.sentence: Have you ever tried Japanese food?
good_examples.audio_text: Have you ever tried Japanese food?

good_examples.note: just = very recently, result is relevant now

good_examples.sentence: I haven't seen that film yet.
good_examples.audio_text: I haven't seen that film yet.
good_examples.note: yet in negatives — not done so far
yet in questions — expecting it to happen

good_examples.sentence: He's lived in Tel Aviv for ten years.
good_examples.audio_text: He's lived in Tel Aviv for ten years.
good_examples.note: for + period — started in past, still true now
since + point in time — started then, still continuing
already = done before expected or before being asked

good_examples.sentence: She's never eaten meat in her life.
good_examples.audio_text: She's never eaten meat in her life.
good_examples.note: never = zero times in whole life up to now

good_examples.sentence: I've lost my keys — has anyone seen them?
good_examples.audio_text: I've lost my keys — has anyone seen them?
good_examples.note: Recent event + present result: keys still missing NOW

good_examples.sentence: This is the best meal I've ever had.
good_examples.audio_text: This is the best meal I've ever had.

good_examples.sentence: Sales have increased a lot this year.
good_examples.audio_text: Sales have increased a lot this year.
good_examples.note: This year = unfinished period → present perfect

bad_examples.wrong: I have been to Paris last year.
bad_examples.correct: I went to Paris last year.
bad_examples.reason: 'Last year' is a specific past time → past simple. Present perfect CANNOT be used with specific past times.
Life experience questions with 'ever' always use present perfect: Have you ever...?
'Since' with a starting point going up to NOW = present perfect, not present simple.

bad_examples.wrong: She has went to the shop.
bad_examples.correct: She has gone to the shop.
bad_examples.reason: go → went (past simple) → gone (past participle). has gone, not has went.

bad_examples.wrong: Have you seen him last week?
bad_examples.correct: Did you see him last week?
bad_examples.reason: 'Last week' = specific past time → past simple always.

exercises.question: I ___ (never / try) Indian food — is it spicy?
exercises.hint: never + life experience → present perfect. never goes between have and the past participle.
exercises.explanation: have never tried — never sits between have and the past participle.
exercises.hebrew_note: מעולם לא + ניסיון בחיים = have never + past participle

exercises.question: She ___ (just / finish) her presentation — she looks relieved.
exercises.hint: just + present perfect for very recent events. she → has.
exercises.explanation: She has just finished — has for she/he/it, just between has and participle.

exercises.question: ___ you ever ___ (be) to Morocco?
exercises.hint: Ever + life experience question → present perfect
exercises.explanation: Have you ever been? — life experience question with ever.

exercises.question: I ___ (not / see) that film yet.
exercises.hint: yet in negatives → present perfect
exercises.explanation: haven't seen yet — not done so far.

exercises.question: He ___ (live) in this city ___ he was born.
exercises.hint: since + point in time + present perfect. he → has.
exercises.explanation: has lived since — started at birth, still living here now.

exercises.question: We ___ (already / book) the restaurant — don't worry.
exercises.hint: already = done before expected. Already goes between have and the participle.
exercises.explanation: have already booked — already in the middle.

exercises.question: I ___ (work) here ___ three years.
exercises.hint: for + period of time to present → present perfect
exercises.explanation: have worked for — duration from past to now.

exercises.question: She looks upset. What ___ (happen)?
exercises.hint: Recent event with current result (she looks upset NOW) → present perfect
exercises.explanation: What has happened? — current relevance.

exercises.question: ___ the postman ___ (come) yet?
exercises.hint: yet in questions. postman = he → has.
exercises.explanation: Has the postman come yet? — expecting the postman.

exercises.question: I ___ (break) my phone — I need to buy a new one.
exercises.hint: Recent event + present result (need a new one NOW)
exercises.explanation: have broken — result is present: I need a new one now.

exercises.question: They ___ (not / decide) where to go on holiday yet.
exercises.hint: Not yet = haven't done it so far → present perfect negative
exercises.explanation: haven't decided yet — not done so far.

exercises.question: It is the best film I ___ (ever / see).
exercises.hint: Superlative + ever → present perfect always
exercises.explanation: The best I have ever seen — superlative + ever = present perfect.

exercises.question: How long ___ you ___ (know) each other?
exercises.hint: How long + from past to now → present perfect
exercises.explanation: How long have you known = asking about duration up to now.

exercises.question: Choose the correct sentence:
exercises.answer: I've never been to the USA.
exercises.hint: Life experience + never → present perfect
exercises.explanation: I've never been — present perfect for life experience.
distractors: I never went to the USA.
I never been to the USA.
I didn't never go to the USA.

exercises.question: ___ you ___ the news today?
exercises.hint: Recent event today with present relevance → present perfect
exercises.explanation: Have you heard the news today? — unfinished day, current relevance.

exercises.question: I ___ in this company since I graduated.
exercises.hint: Since + point in time to NOW → present perfect
exercises.explanation: since I graduated = from that point to now → present perfect.

exercises.question: Which sentence is correct?
exercises.answer: I went to Paris last summer.
exercises.hint: Last summer = specific past time → which tense?
exercises.explanation: 'Last summer' = specific finished time → past simple. Never use present perfect with past time expressions.
distractors: I have gone to Paris last summer.
I have been to Paris last summer.
I've went to Paris last summer.

exercises.question: She ___ already ___ the problem.
exercises.hint: already between has and past participle. she → has.
exercises.explanation: has already solved — already in the middle.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'I have seen him yesterday.'
exercises.hint: Yesterday = specific past time → which tense?
exercises.explanation: yesterday = specific past time → past simple always.
exercises.hebrew_note: yesterday תמיד = past simple, לעולם לא present perfect

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'Did you ever visit Japan?'
exercises.hint: Life experience question with ever → which tense?
exercises.explanation: ever + life experience → present perfect: Have you ever visited?

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'I live here since five years.'
exercises.answer: I have lived here for five years.
exercises.hint: Two things to fix: the tense and the preposition before the number.
exercises.explanation: Duration (five years) uses FOR. Since point in time to now → present perfect.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'She has went to the shop.'
exercises.answer: She has gone to the shop.
exercises.hint: go → past simple = went. go → past participle = ?
exercises.explanation: go → gone (past participle). has gone, never has went.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'Have you seen him last week?'
exercises.answer: Did you see him last week?
exercises.hint: Last week = specific past time → which tense?
exercises.explanation: last week = specific past time → past simple.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.hint: Life experience question: Have + subject + ever + past participle?
exercises.explanation: Have you ever tried? — ever between subject and past participle.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: She has just finished the report.
exercises.hint: just goes between has and the past participle
exercises.explanation: She has just finished — just in the middle.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: I haven't seen him for two weeks.
exercises.hint: Negative present perfect + for + duration
exercises.explanation: I haven't seen him for two weeks — duration since last seeing him.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: This is the best meal I've ever had.
exercises.hint: Superlative + present perfect + ever
exercises.explanation: The best meal I've ever had — superlative triggers present perfect.

speak.sentence: Have you ever been to a country where you didn't speak the language?
chunks: Have you ever been to a country
where you didn't speak the language?

speak.sentence: I've lived in this city for most of my life.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'I've' = eyv — contracted, one smooth syllable. 'Most' = MOHST.

speak.sentence: She's just heard the news and she can't believe it.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'She's' = SHEEZ. 'Heard' = HURD — irregular past participle. 'Believe' = bih-LEEV.

speak.sentence: I haven't seen that film yet — is it any good?
chunks: I haven't seen that film yet —

speak.sentence: We've already decided where to go — we're going to Greece.
chunks: We've already decided where to go —
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Already' = awl-RED-ee — stress on second syllable. 'Greece' = GREES — long ee sound.

speak.sentence: How long have you been working at your current company?
chunks: How long have you been working

speak.sentence: I've never tried scuba diving but I'd really like to one day.
chunks: but I'd really like to one day.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Scuba' = SKOO-buh. 'Diving' = DYV-ing. 'I'd' = eyed — contracted I would.

speak.sentence: Has anyone seen my glasses? I've looked everywhere.
